title: Phylogenetic Trees and the Moduli of n Points
speaker: Herwig Hauser
abstract:
We present a combinatorial approach to the Deligne-Mumford-Knudsen compactification of the moduli space of n distinct points on the projective line P1. The idea is to choose a totally symmetric embedding of the orbits of generic points into a high-dimensional projective scheme and to take the Zariski-closure Xn of the image there. Defining equations for Xn are found via cross-ratios, and phylogenetic trees naturally yield a stratification. The forgetful map Xn+1− greater than Xn exhibits as fibers n-pointed stable curves, which thus arise a posteriori and for free.
If time permits, the intriguing case of points in P2
 will be sketched.

proof_stages.label: Moduli problem for labelled configurations
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up labelled n-point configurations in projective space, generic d+2 subconfigurations with strict PGL transitivity, and in dimension one identifies the open quotient with M_{0,n} while posing the compactification problem.

proof_stages.label: Limit ambiguity motivating symmetrization
proof_stages.summary: Degenerating four- and five-point configurations demonstrate that quotient limits depend on the chosen normalization and on relative rates of approach, so a satisfactory compactification should record all symmetric normalized limits.

proof_stages.label: Symmetrization compactification X_n
proof_stages.summary: For d=1, each triple of labels is normalized to 0,1,∞, producing a symmetric embedding of U_n into a product of strings of n-gons, and X_n is defined as the Zariski closure of the image inside the prescribed-normalization locus Z_n.

proof_stages.label: Cross-ratio equations and basic properties
proof_stages.summary: PGL2-invariant cross-ratios are imposed to define an explicit equation locus Y_n containing X_n, and the speaker records projectivity, dimension n−3, forgetful morphisms, and comparison with the usual compactification of M_{0,n}.

proof_stages.label: Higher-dimensional analogue
proof_stages.summary: The same symmetrization idea is sketched for configurations in P^2 and beyond, where quadruple normalizations, higher cross-ratios, and both incidence and collinearity data replace the one-dimensional collision data.

proof_stages.label: Phylogenetic tree attached to a string
proof_stages.summary: For a string satisfying the equal-cross-ratio equations, the construction takes PGL2-orbits as vertices, complementary incident sets as edges, and singleton labels as leaves, yielding a labelled phylogenetic tree; all such trees arise and stratify the compactification.

proof_stages.label: Local coordinates, smoothness, and stable-curve fibres
proof_stages.summary: Extremal graphs and edge deletions select n−3 cross-ratios that give local charts and recover all remaining cross-ratios, leading to smoothness, normal-crossing boundary structure, and identification of forgetful-map fibres with pointed stable rational curves.

examples.name: Four-point degeneration with two normalizations
examples.note: The family (0,1,∞,t) as t→∞ gives different limiting configurations after different PGL2 normalizations, producing the need to retain all normalized limits rather than a single quotient limit.
examples.engagement_reason: He computes two normalizations of the four-point family as t goes to infinity and compares the resulting distinct limits.

passes.reason: It is a boundary four-point configuration with coincident limits, used to show that a quotient orbit can have inequivalent normalized limits.
A collision limit of four labeled P1-points, used to show that an orbit-family can have different limits under different PGL2 normalizations.
The limiting four-point configurations have coincident points and sit on the boundary, and they are used to show that a quotient family can have two non-equivalent normalized limits.
A boundary four-point degeneration with coincident limiting points, used to show that orbit limits depend on the chosen normalization.
Boundary collision of the fourth point with infinity gives inequivalent normalized limits.
A boundary collision of four marked P1 points gives non-PGL2-equivalent limits under two normalizations, obstructing a naive quotient limit.
a boundary four-point collision used to show that a naive orbit-limit depends on the chosen PGL2 normalization
The four-point family has colliding boundary limits that are inequivalent under two normalizations, obstructing a naive representative-independent limit.
the two normalizations of the four-point family give inequivalent collision limits, obstructing a unique naive quotient limit
A collision boundary limit of four points gives two inequivalent PGL2-normalized limits, obstructing a naive orbit limit.
The same degenerating four-tuple has inequivalent PGL2-normalized boundary limits, showing naive quotient limits are not canonical.
It is a boundary collision of four points used to show that naive orbit limits depend on the chosen normalization.

examples.name: Five-point degeneration with relative rates
examples.note: The two-parameter family with s,t→∞ shows that limits can depend on the ratio t/s, forcing boundary curves rather than only finitely many added points.
examples.engagement_reason: He runs the five-point degeneration with parameters s and t, specializes t=cs, and computes the limiting family depending on c.

passes.reason: It is a boundary five-point degeneration whose limit depends on the rate parameter, obstructing a compactification by only finitely many added points.
A boundary collision of five P1-points whose limit depends on the relative rate t/s, showing that finitely many naive limits are insufficient.
The five-point family collapses several points at infinity with a rate parameter, showing that adding only finitely many point-limits would miss a curve of boundary limits.
A limiting five-point configuration with coalescing points, used to show that relative rates create extra boundary parameters.
Boundary degeneration with s,t→∞ retains the relative rate c as a whole family of limits.
The five-point family tends to a boundary configuration whose limit depends on the relative rate parameter, showing finite or naive limits miss a curve of limits.
a boundary five-point degeneration with collisions whose relative rate parameter shows that finitely many added limits are insufficient
The five-point family degenerates to the boundary with a limit depending on the relative rate parameter, showing that merely adding finitely many point-limits is insufficient.
the five-point degeneration retains the rate parameter c in the limit, showing that finitely many added boundary points are insufficient
A five-point boundary degeneration with several points coalescing has rate-dependent limits, obstructing a finite naive boundary.
The relative rates of s,t→∞ produce a c-family of boundary limits, obstructing a finite or rate-blind compactification.
It is a five-point boundary degeneration whose different relative rates obstruct a finite-point naive compactification.

examples.name: Six-point P2 kite graph
examples.note: The six-orbit incidence/collinearity configuration shows that ordinary singleton point labels no longer supply leaves in dimension two, motivating the replacement by singleton lines indexed by pairs of labels.
examples.engagement_reason: He gives explicit incidence and collinearity data for six P2 orbits, connects the vertices, attaches singleton lines, and obtains the kite graph.

passes.reason: The six-point P2 kite graph comes from incidences/collinearities and is used to show that the higher-dimensional graph need not be a phylogenetic tree with ordinary leaves.
The P2 kite graph encodes special coincidence and collinearity data and is used to show that the d=2 graph need not be a phylogenetic tree.
